paragraphs.speaker: Marcus
paragraphs.en: Come in, take a seat. I'm Marcus, Head of Operations. Thanks for making the time — I know the commute from Bristol can be brutal.
paragraphs.fr: Entrez, asseyez-vous. Je m'appelle Marcus, je suis directeur des opérations. Merci d'avoir trouvé le temps — je sais que le trajet depuis Bristol peut être éprouvant.

paragraphs.speaker: Claire
paragraphs.en: It's no trouble at all, thank you for seeing me. I actually took the first train this morning to make sure I wasn't cutting it too fine.
paragraphs.fr: Ça ne me dérange pas du tout, merci de me recevoir. J'ai pris le premier train ce matin pour m'assurer de ne pas être dans les dernières minutes.

paragraphs.speaker: Marcus
paragraphs.en: Good thinking. Right, I've had a look at your CV and I have to say, your background in supply chain management is impressive. But tell me — why the sudden pivot towards the tech sector?
paragraphs.fr: C'est sage. Bien, j'ai regardé votre CV et je dois dire que votre expérience en gestion de la chaîne d'approvisionnement est impressionnante. Mais dites-moi — pourquoi ce virage soudain vers le secteur technologique ?

paragraphs.speaker: Claire
paragraphs.en: That's a fair question. Honestly, I'd been finding the logistics industry increasingly rigid — same processes, little room for innovation. I wanted to be somewhere where I could actually build things from the ground up, rather than just maintaining existing systems.
paragraphs.fr: C'est une bonne question. Honnêtement, je trouvais le secteur logistique de plus en plus rigide — les mêmes processus, peu de place pour l'innovation. Je voulais être dans un endroit où je pourrais vraiment construire des choses depuis le début, plutôt que de simplement maintenir des systèmes existants.

paragraphs.speaker: Marcus
paragraphs.en: I can appreciate that. And you've done some upskilling in the meantime, I gather? The Python certification caught my eye.
paragraphs.fr: Je comprends ça. Et vous vous êtes formée entre-temps, si je comprends bien ? La certification Python m'a attiré l'œil.

paragraphs.speaker: Claire
paragraphs.en: Yes — I completed it over six months while holding down my previous job. It wasn't always easy to juggle both, but I was determined to prove to myself that I could make the transition.
paragraphs.fr: Oui — je l'ai passée en six mois tout en conservant mon poste précédent. Ce n'était pas toujours facile de gérer les deux à la fois, mais j'étais déterminée à me prouver que je pouvais faire cette transition.

paragraphs.speaker: Marcus
paragraphs.en: Tell me about a time you had to manage a serious setback at work. How did you handle it?
paragraphs.fr: Parlez-moi d'une fois où vous avez dû gérer un sérieux revers au travail. Comment avez-vous géré ça ?

paragraphs.speaker: Claire
paragraphs.en: There was one situation that really tested me. Our main supplier pulled out at the last minute before a major product launch — leaving us holding a very short deadline and no backup plan. I had to think on my feet, get on the phone with three alternative suppliers simultaneously, and negotiate a deal within 48 hours. We pulled it off, just barely.
paragraphs.fr: Il y a eu une situation qui m'a vraiment mise à l'épreuve. Notre principal fournisseur s'est retiré à la dernière minute avant un lancement de produit majeur — nous laissant avec un délai très court et aucun plan de secours. J'ai dû improviser, appeler trois fournisseurs alternatifs en même temps, et négocier un accord en 48 heures. On y est arrivés, de justesse.

paragraphs.speaker: Marcus
paragraphs.en: Impressive. And how do you handle criticism? I should warn you — this team gives feedback pretty bluntly. We don't have time to sugarcoat things.
paragraphs.fr: Impressionnant. Et comment gérez-vous les critiques ? Je dois vous prévenir — cette équipe donne son feedback de façon assez directe. On n'a pas le temps de tourner autour du pot.

paragraphs.speaker: Claire
paragraphs.en: Honestly, I welcome it. I've always found vague feedback far more frustrating than direct criticism. If someone tells me clearly what isn't working, I can fix it. What I can't work with is ambiguity.
paragraphs.fr: Honnêtement, je le recherche. J'ai toujours trouvé un feedback vague bien plus frustrant qu'une critique directe. Si quelqu'un me dit clairement ce qui ne fonctionne pas, je peux le corriger. Ce avec quoi je ne peux pas travailler, c'est l'ambiguïté.

paragraphs.speaker: Marcus
paragraphs.en: Last question — where do you see yourself in five years?
paragraphs.fr: Dernière question — où vous voyez-vous dans cinq ans ?

paragraphs.speaker: Claire
paragraphs.en: Leading a team, ideally. I don't want to be a senior individual contributor forever — I'm genuinely driven by the challenge of developing people and building something bigger than myself. I'd like to think that in five years, I'd have built a function here, not just filled a role.
paragraphs.fr: À la tête d'une équipe, idéalement. Je ne veux pas rester indéfiniment une collaboratrice individuelle senior — ce qui me motive vraiment, c'est le défi de développer des personnes et de construire quelque chose de plus grand que moi. J'aimerais penser que dans cinq ans, j'aurais construit une fonction ici, pas seulement occupé un poste.

vocabulary.definition_en: le trajet quotidien entre le domicile et le travail
vocabulary.example: The commute from Bristol to London takes about 90 minutes by train.
The traffic this morning was brutal — I sat in the same spot for 40 minutes.

vocabulary.definition_en: un changement de direction significatif, surtout dans la carrière ou les affaires
vocabulary.example: After ten years in finance, she made a pivot to the creative industries.

vocabulary.definition_en: incapable de s'adapter ou de changer ; inflexible
vocabulary.example: The management was too rigid to consider any new approaches.

vocabulary.definition_en: acquisition de nouvelles compétences, surtout pour améliorer ses perspectives de carrière
vocabulary.example: With AI transforming every sector, upskilling has become essential.

vocabulary.definition_en: gérer plusieurs tâches ou responsabilités en même temps
vocabulary.example: She was juggling a full-time job, night classes, and two young children.

vocabulary.definition_en: un problème qui retarde ou empêche la progression
vocabulary.example: Losing their main investor was a major setback for the startup.

vocabulary.definition_en: se retirer d'un accord ou d'une situation
vocabulary.example: The investor pulled out of the deal at the last minute.

vocabulary.definition_en: de manière directe et franche, sans adoucir le message
vocabulary.example: To put it bluntly, your presentation was not ready.

vocabulary.definition_en: la qualité d'être ouvert à plus d'une interprétation ; manque de clarté
vocabulary.example: In a crisis, ambiguity is dangerous — people need clear instructions.

grammar_points.title: Conditional modals for polite distancing: 'I'd' in formal speech
grammar_points.explanation: En anglais professionnel britannique, utiliser **'I'd' (I would)** au lieu de 'I' ajoute une couche de politesse et de prudence. Cela montre que vous formulez une opinion réfléchie plutôt qu'une déclaration directe. Très utile en entretien et dans les présentations formelles.
examples: 'I'd like to think that in five years, I'd have built a function here.' (polite / thoughtful)
vs. 'I will build a function here.' (too assertive, potentially arrogant)
'I'd say the biggest challenge is communication.' → professional, measured
'I'd hope to be leading a team by then.' → expressing ambition humbly

grammar_points.title: Gerunds after prepositions
grammar_points.explanation: En anglais, quand un **verbe suit une préposition**, il doit être à la **forme en -ing (gérondif)**. C'est une source d'erreurs très courante pour les francophones, qui pourraient être tentés d'utiliser un infinitif.
examples: I was determined to prove myself... → infinitive after 'determined to' ✓
without *seeing* the full picture / by *asking* the right questions
French trap: 'avant de faire' = 'before *doing*' (not 'before to do')

grammar_points.title: The STAR method in English: past tenses for professional storytelling
grammar_points.explanation: Lorsqu'on répond à des questions d'entretien avec un exemple concret (Situation, Tâche, Action, Résultat), l'anglais utilise le **Simple Past** pour les actions terminées et le **Past Perfect** pour le contexte. Remarquez comment Claire structure sa réponse : elle pose le contexte au past perfect ('our supplier *had* pulled out') et raconte l'histoire au simple past.
examples: 'There was one situation that really tested me.' → sets the scene (simple past)
'Our main supplier pulled out at the last minute.' → the key event (simple past)
'I had to think on my feet.' → immediate reaction (simple past)
'We pulled it off, just barely.' → result (simple past)

expressions.meaning_fr: arriver de justesse, être dans les dernières secondes
expressions.example: I almost missed the train — I really cut it fine this time.
expressions.note: Expression britannique. Similaire à 'just in time' mais avec plus de tension implicite.

expressions.example: We built this company from the ground up — no investors, no safety net.
expressions.note: Souligne que quelque chose a été construit entièrement à partir de zéro, sans rien de donné au préalable.

expressions.meaning_fr: improviser, réagir vite sans avoir le temps de se préparer
expressions.example: Good teachers know how to think on their feet when a lesson goes off plan.
expressions.note: Implique une réflexion rapide et adaptative en temps réel, sans préparation.

expressions.meaning_fr: édulcorer, atténuer une mauvaise nouvelle, tourner autour du pot
expressions.example: I won't sugarcoat it — the results were disappointing.
expressions.note: Souvent utilisé à la forme négative : 'I'm not going to sugarcoat this...' pour signaler une honnêteté brutale.

expressions.meaning_fr: réussir quelque chose de difficile ou d'improbable
expressions.example: Nobody thought they could win, but they pulled it off.
expressions.note: Implique la réussite de quelque chose d'impressionnant contre toute attente. 'We pulled it off, just barely.' = succès, mais de justesse.

written.question: Why did Claire decide to change sectors?
options: She was made redundant from her logistics job.
She found logistics too rigid with little room for innovation.
She had always dreamed of working in tech.
The salary in tech is significantly higher.
written.explanation: Claire says she was 'finding the logistics industry increasingly rigid — same processes, little room for innovation'.

written.question: What does Marcus mean when he says the team 'doesn't sugarcoat things'?
options: The team doesn't use sweet language.
The team gives feedback very directly, without softening it.
The team is not polite in meetings.
written.explanation: 'To sugarcoat' means to soften bad news. Marcus is warning Claire that feedback is blunt and direct.

written.sentence: I've always found it hard to ________ my personal life and my professional ambitions.
written.hint: Think of the verb meaning 'to manage multiple things at the same time'.

written.sentence: She ________ of the project at the last minute, leaving the whole team in a difficult position.
To ________, the plan was poorly designed from the start.
written.hint: A common expression used to introduce a frank, direct opinion.

written.french: Je ne vais pas édulcorer les choses : le projet a été un échec total.
written.suggested_answer: I'm not going to sugarcoat it: the project was a complete failure.

written.french: Elle a dû improviser lorsque le principal fournisseur s'est retiré à la dernière minute.
written.suggested_answer: She had to think on her feet when the main supplier pulled out at the last minute.

written.french: Personne ne pensait qu'ils y arriveraient, mais ils ont réussi, de justesse.
written.suggested_answer: Nobody thought they could pull it off, but they managed it, just barely.

oral.instruction: Read Claire's final answer aloud as naturally as possible, as if you were in a real interview.
oral.text: Leading a team, ideally. I don't want to be a senior individual contributor forever — I'm genuinely driven by the challenge of developing people and building something bigger than myself. I'd like to think that in five years, I'd have built a function here, not just filled a role.
oral.focus: Pay attention to the rhythm of the contrast: 'not just filled a role' should have emphasis on 'built' and 'filled'.

oral.question: Tell me about a time you had to handle a stressful or unexpected situation. Use the STAR method.
oral.suggested_answer: There was a situation that really tested me during my final year of studies. Our group project supervisor pulled out two weeks before the deadline without any explanation, leaving us without a mentor. I had to think on my feet: I reached out to two other professors, explained the situation clearly, and found a replacement within three days. We submitted on time, and actually received one of the highest marks in the cohort.

oral.question: How do you personally respond to criticism at work or school? Be honest and use vocabulary from this lesson.
oral.suggested_answer: I think I generally handle criticism well, though it depends on how it's delivered. I genuinely prefer blunt, direct feedback to vague comments — ambiguity is far more frustrating than honesty. I've been known to get slightly defensive initially, but I've learnt to sit with criticism and reflect on it before reacting. I'd rather someone didn't sugarcoat things — I can handle the truth.
